attack_surface.exposure: Hash and Verify action handlers accept arbitrary user data, algorithm selection, and hex-encoded hashes
attack_surface.trust_boundary: All inputs come from .pr files (user-authored). External data enters only if the user pipes it in via variables.
mitigations: Null checks on Data and Hash parameters
FormatException catch on hex decoding
Algorithm validated via switch expression — unsupported returns error, not exception
Providers return Data, never throw — behavior methods contract enforced

attack_surface.exposure: Type-keyed ConcurrentDictionary allowing any code to register/replace module providers
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Same assembly boundary — only PLang assembly code and user-loaded libraries can access Engine.Providers
mitigations: ConcurrentDictionary is thread-safe
Generic type constraints (where T : class) prevent value type registration
No providers registered in production code yet — only consumed via GetOrDefault with hardcoded defaults
gaps: No audit trail for provider registration — a replaced provider is silently overwritten (by design for user-sovereign model)

attack_surface.exposure: Bool property controlling trust decisions on received Data
attack_surface.trust_boundary: internal SetVerified — only same-assembly code can set it
mitigations: { get; private set; } prevents external assignment
internal SetVerified limits to same assembly
[JsonIgnore] prevents deserialization from setting it
Not yet connected to actual crypto verification — placeholder
gaps: Any code in PLang assembly can call SetVerified — no crypto proof required (acceptable: assembly = trusted boundary)

mitigations: SensitivePropertyFilter strips [Sensitive] properties from JsonStreamSerializer output
Data.Envelope._envelopeJsonOptions includes SensitivePropertyFilter.Filter (fixed since identity branch)
Storage serialization (DataSource) correctly preserves sensitive data for persistence
gaps: Dot navigation (%MyIdentity.PrivateKey%) still resolves — by design, user-sovereign
Raw JsonSerializer.Serialize (outside stream serializer) would include sensitive data — no known call sites for identity objects

attack_surface.exposure: Ed25519 key generation, storage, export, rename, archive operations
attack_surface.trust_boundary: All operations are user-initiated via .pr files. Keys stored in actor's SQLite DataSource.
mitigations: Parameterized SQL queries — no injection vectors
Name validation (non-empty) on create
Uniqueness check across all identities including archived
Rename is atomic (save-new then remove-old)
Archive prevents setting as default
Export handler returns raw key on explicit user request (user-sovereign)
gaps: Ed25519 byte arrays not zeroed after encoding (managed runtime limitation)
Private key stored as plain base64 string in SQLite (accepted risk — disk encryption is user's responsibility)

findings.vector: DefaultProvider.Verify uses Span<byte>.SequenceEqual which short-circuits on first byte mismatch, enabling timing side-channel attacks. An attacker making repeated verification requests can determine the expected hash byte-by-byte by measuring response times.
findings.preconditions: PLang app exposes a verification endpoint to external clients (e.g., HTTP API that verifies submitted data against stored hashes). Attacker can make many requests with controlled timing measurement.
findings.impact: Attacker can recover the expected hash value without knowing the original data. For integrity verification (not password hashing), this reveals the hash but not necessarily the pre-image.
findings.exploit_sketch: 1. Send verify requests with hash = 00...00 (all zeros), vary first byte until timing increases. 2. Lock first byte, vary second byte. 3. Repeat for all 32 bytes. Each byte requires ~256 attempts with statistical significance. Total: ~8192 requests for a 256-bit hash.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `actual.AsSpan().SequenceEqual(expectedHash)` with `CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals(actual, expectedHash)`. This is a one-line change. CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals is available in .NET 6+.

findings.vector: Ed25519 private key byte arrays from NSec.Cryptography are not explicitly zeroed after base64 encoding in KeyGenerator.cs. The byte[] remains in managed memory until GC collects it.
findings.preconditions: Attacker has memory read access to the process (e.g., via debugger, memory dump, or cold boot attack).
findings.impact: Private key material may be recoverable from process memory after key generation.
findings.exploit_sketch: Dump process memory, scan for Ed25519 key patterns (32-byte aligned, high entropy).
findings.proposed_fix: Inherent limitation of .NET managed runtime — Array.Clear helps but GC can still copy. Accept as managed runtime risk. Consider using SecureString or platform-specific secure memory in future if high-assurance key protection is needed.

findings.vector: Private key stored as unprotected base64 string in IdentityVariable and persisted to SQLite. [Sensitive] attribute only filters serialization output, not storage.
findings.preconditions: Attacker has file system access to .db/system.sqlite.
findings.impact: All identity private keys are readable from the database file.
findings.proposed_fix: By design — user-sovereign model. The user owns their disk. Disk encryption is the user's responsibility. Document that .db/ contains sensitive key material.

findings.vector: Engine.Providers allows silent provider replacement — Register<T> overwrites without logging or notification. A library loaded via library.load could replace the crypto provider with a weak implementation.
findings.preconditions: User loads an untrusted library that calls engine.Providers.Register<ICryptoProvider>(weakProvider).
findings.impact: All subsequent crypto operations use the weak provider. Hash verification could be bypassed.
findings.exploit_sketch: Ship a library DLL that registers a crypto provider whose Verify() always returns true.
findings.proposed_fix: Not a vulnerability — user-sovereign model. The user chose to load the library. library.load already grants full trust. Consider adding a debug-level log when a provider is replaced, for transparency.

summary: Crypto module is well-designed with clean separation between handlers (thin) and providers (pluggable). One medium finding: timing side-channel in DefaultProvider.Verify using SequenceEqual instead of CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals. Three low/accepted-risk findings consistent with managed runtime and user-sovereign threat model. Identity module carries forward from prior PASS audit with no regressions. Data.Envelope now correctly includes SensitivePropertyFilter. Overall posture: PASS — the timing fix is recommended but not blocking given the threat model.
